channel.title: Fallout on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Fallout on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Fallout vs. Arcanum Story States
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Fallout versus Arcanum’s story states.

This is something I’ve mentioned many, many times on here. I always talk about Fallout having three and Arcanum having 27. Most recently, Uh, I did it in a video and Richard to the Argo asked: “Hey Tim, can you do a video breaking down what a 27 act story looks like? You’ve mentioned it a few times now and I can’t quite wrap my head around that number.

item.title: Fallout: Destiny Or Preparation?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about whether Fallout was destined to happen or the result of massive preparation.

This came from a conversation I had with Matt Bal in his podcast, The Sewers of Paris. I will link it below.

It’s a really good podcast and I’m a fan of Matt’s YouTube channel because he talks a lot about LGBT representation in books and movies and TV shows way long time ago and back. I mean, he does modern day, but he goes really far back and shows how there were always gay people working in a lot of the movies and TV shows that you liked.

item.title: A Typical Day Of Making Fallout
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about a typical day of making Fallout, kind of to give you an insight into what it was like.

And I’m going to do it in the form of a musical. A typical day of making fall. No, we’re not going to do that Scratch that, I’m just going to talk about it. This time I’m going to talk about, to me, a typical day for making Fallout. It’s going to be about a year or so into development.

item.title: Non-Expository Fallout Lore
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, for a fun Friday, I wanted to talk about nonexpository Fallout lore.

Let me explain what I mean by that. First of all, I’m talking about lore from the first game only, not even from Fallout 2.. I’m talking about lore that the original team discussed among ourselves and decided was true, and then we made the game based on that. We never wrote this stuff down. In fact, there weren’t a lot of design documents back in the 90s. I think the vision document was one of the few truly laid out, organized, specific documents we wrote.

item.title: Fallout Lore Exploration
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Fallout lore exploration.

This comes from a really good question from Joseé Luis Lopez 7511, who asked: “What part of the Fallout universe needs to be expanded upon or what story or stories deserve to be continued? Perhaps take a bit from each game and weave the evolution of the formula”. This is an interesting question. Um, I debated whether to answer it because whenever I talk about Fallout, some people go crazy. But there’s a lot of me in Fallout. I’ve said this a lot.

item.title: Fallout's Memory Model
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Fallout’s memory model.

I had a good time talking about the foot sliding problem in the previous video and that was kind of where programming and art came together. This talk is purely programming. It is how we addressed memory use in Fallout — Fallout one. For those of you who are using numbers, It’s a an incredibly good example of game development in the 90s. In fact, it’s such a 90s story that if you listen carefully in the background you can probably hear Germany reunifying, what you need to know as context. Well, I’m going to give a lot of context.

item.title: Fallout Foot Sliding: A Case Study in R&D
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Fallout Footsliding, which I think is a good case study of R&D, especially in game development in the 1980s and 1990s.

I’ve talked about the Fallout foot sliding problem a lot in a number of different videos. Most recently I did it in a video about the difference between first and third person And Koala Brownie asked if I would be said: “I would be interested to know why the foot slip took so much programming time. If there’s any story to tell As a TV animator, that would seem like more of an art issue”. You’re right, and but I want to talk about it I have to give a lot of context before I dive into the exact problem, which was Thorny And I decided to make a video because people have asked about this foot sighting problem a lot, because I bring it up a lot and I wanted a video that I could point to and go here I talk about it all.

item.title: Challenges Making A Fallout Remaster
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the challenges making of making a Fallout remaster.

I’m doing this on a fun Friday because I think it’ll be fun for you guys. It may not be fun for me because I had to think about this a lot and write a bunch of stuff down.

Now I’ve already done a video, and I’ll link it below, about REM, the remaking Fallout, or Fallout one or OG Fallout or whatever people want to refer to it is. I call the first fallout Fallout. That right there, by the way, is an issue, because people argue over what to call the first Fallout. I’ve been sitting on this video for a while because I did the one called the Remake and a lot of people were like no, that’s really, you’re talking about a remaster. So I want to be clear here.

item.title: Alternate Fallout Radiation Design
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about an alternate Fallout radiation design.

It’s a fun Friday. I mentioned last week in my video on my favorite shipped designs that I had an alternate radiation design for Fallout, but because it never — I never shipped that — it’s not in that list. It also wasn’t rejected.

In fact, this is something I made years later, because, full disclosure, I was never 100% satisfied with how we did radiation in Fallout. It was just damage, um, but later, fallouts — I was also not 100% satisfied. It became a DOT or a Max Health loss or something that was kind of like poison. I didn’t like any of this, so years later — and I’m not 100% sure when, because it’s not dated — it was after I left interplay. It might have even been after fall three came out. I don’t know. It was on loose leaf, undated paper — I came up with a different design that I would have loved to have used, so I thought I’d talk.

item.title: Real-Time Fallout
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about realtime Fallout.

Gkid 100 asked: can you make a video about what Fallout would have been like if you did make it realtime action? What would have been needed to come out of the game? What about the balance of the perks would have changed? Do you think multiplayer could have worked in the Fallout Universe? I’ve talked about remaking Fallout and also what would change today in two separate videos and I’ll link them below. So I just want to make it clear off the top that I would not have made Fallout real time, not back in 97, not today, in 2025, and this is despite — and I’ve mentioned this — interplay marketing approached me in 1996, a year before we came out, and they wanted Fallout to be me made real time because of Diablo. This they pushed several times and the way I finally got it — the stop — was just pointing out how much money I would need and time, and then they finally.

item.title: Why I Wanted Luck In Fallout
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about why I wanted luck in Fallout.

It’s for me early February, I’ve been snowed in for a few days and this is a fun Friday video and I figured why not? If you remember, I talked about when Fallout had to be converted from gerps to special. Chris Taylor had a home brew system he wanted to use, which we later added perks to when Brian Fargo said: hey, I want something for people to buy, besid skills, but the attributes were just strength, perception, endurance, Charisma, intelligence and Agility. There was no luck I I talk about this in the how we made Fallout special vid. I really, really wanted luck to be in there because I had had a ton of fun with luck based characters in gerps, especially GP supers, and in Champions and in a few other tabletop role playing games that had luck in them. Um, if you watch my interplay after hours video, I talk about a character I had called lucky boy who bought all the different luck, uh, advantages or powers that were in the tabletop I was using and was just insanely fun to play. It wasn’t that he was good at anything, it’s just he frequently got lucky and scored critical hits or Critical with the skill and then anyone who tried to attack him frequently messed up and hurt themselves and other people around them. It was fun, because of that. I wanted in Fallout, I wanted and something like that. First of all, I wanted it as an attribute, because I remember when I brought it up people were like, well, we’re just throw in perk, a luck perk and I’m like, no, as a perk that means some people won’t have it. So when you go, what’s the luck on them?

item.title: Photos From Fallout Development (1994-1997)
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about a collection of Fallout photos I have from 1994 through 1997.

It’s a fun Friday. I dug these up. I figured I’d show them here now. I’ve done two videos with photos on them. One was scenes from a life in game development.

The other was snapshot throughout my career and they’re below. I also did a Fallout timeline. I’ll link that below.

item.title: Fallout's Starting Date
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about fallout’s starting date and specifically what I mean by that is: why is the original Fallout set 84 years after the war?

Now I remember us talking about this. I remember there being a meeting where we discussed it. Because I get into as much as I can remember from that discussion, because I remember taking notes, but I’ve gone through everything I’ve even had, have digitized my handwritten notes that I mostly had at home and there isn’t a writeup on that meeting now. It was probably — destroyed because interplay threatened me with a lawsuit after I left so I destroyed all of the stuff I had at home. I talked about how I took a CD that had Fallout code. It had g code, it had the initial graphics Library code that I built ganol from which I used for the gerp star system generator that I’ve talked about, and it probably had digitized meeting notes on it that thing was destroyed. It’s gone if there’s any record on it, was probably on my interplay computer, which by now is certainly destroyed. I heard it was found at some point. Maybe the notes are out there, so who knows?

item.title: A Real Life Fallout Story With Lasers
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to tell you a real life Fallout story with lasers.

Now, this is something it just happened last month, just a few weeks old, and during this story I will say the phrase: she used her foot to shoot me in the face with a laser, and it will make sense. So let me tell. Let me tell you the uh preamble to the story.

item.title: Pros And Cons Of GURPS As A CRPG
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the pros and cons of gerps as a computer role playing game, specifically what I learned trying to implement gerps as a computer game for Fallout.

This is to answer a question from player one fanatic who asks about, quote, giving a deep dive on gerps that was originally in Fallout. What skills, advantages, disadvantages and other game mechanics were implemented?

item.title: Two Recent Mementos
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about two recent momentos I now have — and they’re behind me, and I’ll Point them out in a few minutes.

This is a Friday fun day so I thought I’d do something fun. I’ve had a lot of people ask about all the momentos that are in these two cabinets — memorabilia from my career days — and I’ve talked about a few of them. I talked about the Fallout foam core board, the really big one.

item.title: Fallout's Effect On Me
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about fallout’s effect on me, which seems like a very personal, self-reflected, reflective, almost narcissistic topic.

But I’m answering someone’s question. Super duper asks: did you ever feel like sometimes your career was sort of defined by Fallout, like obviously it was your what we would call, in the film industry, breakout role, with it being such a massively successful and popular game? But do you ever feel like being tied to Fallout held you back or gave you opportunities you otherwise would not have had, and just for funsies, with it being such an impactful and influential game? Would you care toine on a world wherein Fallout had been cancelled and you think they might have, and how you think that might have affected our culture?

item.title: The Number Of Vaults In Fallout
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the number of vaults in Fallout.

It’s Friday, fun day, so I want to talk about some fun. This is just me talking about It’s not canon — things I say aren’t Canon, but I thought it’ be fun to revisit this after my video months ago about the Vault being used as experiments, which I’ll get to. So the original design for vaults was that there was going to be a thousand, and the only reason this pin was even placed in the original game was when Leonard was doing the Vault suit. He asked me how many digits he’d need to keep on the back, because we had vault 13. But he wanted to know, you know, are they going to be Vault? You know 106, is there going to be Vault?

item.title: Would I Work On Fallout Again?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to answer the question: would I work on Fallout again?

This: in the last few months this question has overtaken my most frequently Asked question — how do I get a job in the game industry — which I have made a video with an answer and then a video about the answer. So I’ve done — how to get a job in the game industry — and then making a demo which is the answer to that. So I want to answer. What I work on followed again, but I have to realize, you have to realize the answer is complicated, but not because of what you think. First of all, there’s a lot of different questions being asked. There.

item.title: The Canceling of Fallout Van Buren...And Me
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the cancellation of Fallout Van Buren and me.

I was watching a documentary — and I’ll link it below — on the development of interplace Fallout by bun, which was 2B, Fallout 3 — it was going to be their first 3D engine Fallout and what happened? And it was a great documentary and I was like Wow, someone’s collected a lot of the facts all in one place, but there was one fact missing that I expected to be missing, which was my involvement in its cancellation. Now this is a story I’ve held off on saying. It’s like what I’m more than a year into this Channel and I held it off for a few reasons.

item.title: Fallout As A Work Of Art
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Fallout as a work of art, and just before I get started, I’m talking about the whole series.

I’m talking about all the games and the TV show. I’m talking about it as an IP. Um, I’ve already done a video about games as art. I’ll link it below. You can kind of see this video as a continuation of that video, because I’m taking some of my ideas from that video and I’m kind of talking about it in more detail. Um, also, I want to clarify that making a game, actual game development — not the end product, but the development that itself — is both an art and a science, and I mean I this is something that is behind all the videos I do, and I’m constantly talking about this, so I’m not going to rehash it, but I will say this: people who try to treat Tre, the development process, like it was Art, are shocked when money factors in, are like: oh my.

item.title: Fallout And Wasteland
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Fallout and Wasteland.

Not Wasteland 2 or three, I’m talking about the original 1988 Wasteland. The reason I want to talk about this is I see a lot of confusing comments online about the influence that Wasteland had on Fallout. Some people think it was a direct influence. Some people think it was a really strong influence. Some people say I said it was an influence, so let me talk about that. I did briefly touch on this in a video I did on a link it in the description on Fallout influences and very clearly in there I say Wasteland was an influence, but I also say it wasn’t strongly an influence. Um, so let me go into more detail about that and right now I can just say, if people go, was wayon an influence on Fallout?

item.title: Fallout Easter Eggs In Other Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Fallout, Easter eggs in other games.

I had a lot of fun doing a video a few months ago about Easter eggs in games in my games that referred, like um, to things we were talking about, like hidden Easter eggs in my games. But I also wanted to talk about times when I found Fallout being referenced in other people’s games. So I’m going to skip games that had Fallout stuff that was overtly connected, like you can buy Fallout skins in Minecraft, um, the new Magic, the Gathering cards that are Fallout based. I mean those, aren’t Easter eggs, those are overtly based on Fallout. But I’m also going to skip games from related companies.

item.title: Fallout TV Show Review
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me Tim. Let’s do a Fallout TV show review.

First off, there’s going to be spoilers. I don’t know exactly what.

I’m spitballing a lot of this, I’m just popping off the top of my esophagus. So I’m just going to say in advance: spoilers are coming up. If you haven’t finished the show, stop, come back when you finished it. Also, I know I’ve said multiple times that I don’t review things. I don’t like to review other people’s work, but I’m going to talk about the show. A lot of people are asking and at the end I’m going to link you to a review that is better than mine. That I watched, I liked a lot, I agreed a lot with it. So you can just go watch that one and go, okay, um, also, because I know I have a tendency to ramble, I’ll just say right now: I like the show there. For people who don’t want ramblings, you can just stop leave. Tim said he liked the show, there you go.

item.title: Fallout TV Show
item.description:
Hi everyone, it’s me, Tim. Okay, let’s talk about the Fallout TV Show, because apparently my game development stories are not interesting to you people, so let me talk about it.

Um, I’m going to have a lot of context for this. I got invited to the Hollywood Premiere, which was on April 9th. Got invited by Todd Howard. He emailed me, asked if I wanted to go. Uh, this is on March 26. I said totally yes. Um, uh, James Alman. Uh, hooked me up with the agency that was doing the tickets.

item.title: Fallout Development Timeline
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to do the Fallout development timeline.

I’ve been putting together notes — I’ve mentioned this before about the making a Fallout and I think I’ve created a pretty definitive timeline here. So it’s kind of long. It’s probably going to be kind of long. There are a few pictures, but not a lot.

So if you need to go get coffee or you need to go to the bathroom, do it now. Don’t pause me later. I’ll know if you pause me, but this is what I put together from probably like five years of notes. Um, maybe a little more. Let me start a little pre Fallout, which is important.

item.title: Fallout 1 and 2 Box Covers
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the Fallout 1 and two box covers and yes, I’m calling fallout Fallout one, just so I don’t say I’m talking about the Fallout box covers and then everyone will get confused.

Now I’ve mentioned this here and there. I know I talked about it on the why I left Fallout 2 video. I think it came up in chats with Leonard, so I’ll link those below if you want even more context. But I wanted to pull, them out, pull out that discussion and make one particular video about it. And I wanted to call it out because otherwise this is the kind of thing I think gets lost in development.

item.title: The Four Steps For Fixing Design Problems
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about fixing design problems.

These are not bugs. I’m not talking about the game is, you know, near in completion and there are things that aren’t working right. I’m talking about you make a design, give it to your programmers and they’re like we gotcha, and they implement it and the artists all go: here’s your good stuff, put it in, here’s everything you asked for and it’s all in and working exactly the way you asked. But there’s a problem, now I’m going to use an example from the original Fallout game — uh, design problem that it shipped with, so I can use that as an example as we’re going through. So in the original Fallout you could buy an energy weapon skill. In fact, you could tag it right at the beginning of the game if you wanted to. The only problem is energy weapons aren’t available for at least the first half and closer to the first 2/3 of the game.

item.title: The Origin of VATS
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the origin of Vats in Fallout and I’ll start by saying we didn’t call it bats in Fallout 1 and two.

That’s a Fallout 3 term, but it’s just what everybody calls it. Now, and this is me answering a question from Vladimir in Germany who asks: I always wonder how this feature that was later known as Vats came to be. Where did it come from?

item.title: Multiple Quest Solutions
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about multiple Quest Solutions and I want to use an example from Fallout that I used in my talk at the reboot conference in 2017.

So that is rescuing Tandy from the Raiders. So this is the map. It happens on very simple map. I should point out one building couple tents — sorry, it’s shown here — after killing some guards or some Raiders.

item.title: The Story Of The Bag
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to tell a story about a picture that floats around on the internet of me with a paper bag on my head sitting at my desk.

It’s this picture here, and as with everything, there is a story behind it and I know um people say they like hearing my stories, so this will test that. And also I’m directly answering or replying to l r I n fi loreny who said: for some reason I keep seeing a picture of you sitting at your desk with a paper bag on your head. There must be a fun story behind that one. There is, so let me give you some Contex text for this story. I’ve said — and you can watch the other Fallout videos, this was during Fallout.

item.title: Mementos: My T. Ray Isaac Original
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about another momento story.

People have asked about stuff in the those uh display cases behind me and I wanted to talk about one of my favorite things, This is me drawn in the style of Fallout of Vault boy by Tay Isaac. This is a Tay Isaac one-of aind original.

I put it in plastic so it doesn’t get damaged. You can see it’s signed by tray. Right there says tray 997.

item.title: The Fallout Team
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about the Fallout team.

By the way, yes, I know it’s dark, got a busy day pre-dawn, blah, blah. The reason I want to talk about the Fallout team: yeah, my Memoirs. That writing didn’t go well. I’m toying with a making of Fallout writing. Uh, I’ve got about 80- 87 Pages.

If I have my glasses on, I’m going to read from it. But then I was reading through it I just got all the warm fuzzies about the Fallout team. It was such a good team and I want to talk about them as individuals. And then I want to talk about a fun thing that happened that I found um in an old journal entry I wrote, but I put together a list of what I consider to be the core set of personnel who worked on Fallout.

item.title: Fallout 4 Launch Party
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the Fallout 4 launch party.

I had a lot of fun talking about the Fallout 3 a press event and launch party, and I got invited to the Fallout 4 launch party, which was again up in LA. Um, I got an email from Todd Howard while I was at obsidian working on tyranny and he said he’d love to have me at the launch party, and this time I got a ticket with a plus one, so I took Robert up with me. It was the party was on November 5th 2015.. So five days before Fallout 4 shipped. So that was really cool.

item.title: Fallout: The Original Intro Story
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about the original story intro to Fallout.

Let me explain, and I’m kind of excited. So if you watched the video where I’m talking with Scott Campbell, he mentioned that he thought the Vault idea was mine. I thought the Vault idea grew out of our idea for making the game Alien Invasion and that people had hidden in vaults. But he said I remember you coming up with the idea for the vault after the brainstorming and that you even said you dreamed about it.

item.title: Fallout 3: Press Event and Launch Party
item.description:
Hey everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Fallout 3 and the E3 showing and the ship party

I got to attend. I was reminded of this when, yesterday, I was talking about not going to Fallout 2’s ship party, and it made me go look up my notes, because I know I went to Fallout 3s and it was an amazing party and I will tell about it. We even got some pictures, but the reason I’m wearing my glasses today is I found my notes and, rather than talk about those two things, I want to read you my notes on them because I want to put you into the headspace I was in. These were written back at the time and so I will start with the dates on them. So, and before I start, let me just say that you know, it was really, really cool that you know I this was started off in 2007, so it’s 10 years after Fallout 1 had shipped. I thought was very cool that I got. Invited to these things, so once again I’m going to thank Todd Howard and Pete Hines for extending this invitation. So first let me start with the Fallout 3 E3 showing.

item.title: Mementos: The Fallout Foamcore Board
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about one of the items behind me.

I get a lot of questions and requests to walk through my bookshelf and my display cases with all the things that are in and there are a lot of things and at first I was surprised, because most of them are what I would call a tchotchke, which are the little things that come with games, and they’re just things — one or two items from every game I’ve made. But I can see why they’d be interesting. Um, to me it’s just the, you know, Museum of my career. But also it’s a lot of stuff I had when I was a kid that I just kept because they influenced my games. But one of the reasons I haven’t been doing videos on these — because I’m gonna do a video on one of them, the Fallout foam core sign, and I’ll tell you it’s because I know this is just the way my brain works.

item.title: Thoughts On A Fallout Remake
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to answer a question from Evan shantlin Who, full disclosure, worked on QA on Fallout.

And Evan asks: hey, Tim, ever thought about what changes or updates you’d like to see in a Fallout remake? Would you want a fully explorable map or Base building added to the game? Are there errors you’d like to see completely reworked or just flushed out?

item.title: Fallout: Nuka Break
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about Fallout — Nuka break.

Fallout new break is a fan made Fallout based series — web series that was made by wayside creations.

Let me talk about how I got involved and then what it was like, because this was a really, really neat thing for me to experience, as someone who’s only ever done game development. Being able to watch and even participate a little bit in a Film Production was amazing. So all of it started in October of 2011 in an interview with the game creators Vault, and I’ll link that below. They were interviewing Chris Avalon — he invited me to come because we’re talking about Fallout — and Zach finfrock, who was at Wayside creations and was kind of spearheading the whole, uh, fallout — Nuka break idea. This was an early October 2011. It was right before I actually joined the company, so I was. I drove down from home. It was after I was well, after a month after I left carbine, but I hadn’t started. Obsidian it, in fact after doing this shoot. I think that’s when I talked to them about coming on board.

item.title: Fallout Regrets
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to answer a question from commenter Brian Eno 608 better be a real name, don’t taunt me like that.

Anyway, Brian Eno asks: what’s one of your biggest Fallout related regrets, specifically story-wise, or a feature you could never get working in on time? Okay, I’m going to assume you mean development of Fallout and not like I regret walking away from the haip. So I’m going to break that into two parts because I have a different answer for Fallout one than I do for Fallout 2. Um, so for Fallout one, my biggest regret is companion AI. I’ve talked about this in another video, but companions were added to the game so late in development that there was very little time to get any AI in for them. When they were first put in, they were handled via script and I think they basically just followed the player around and shared any hostility flag that the player would have. So anything mad at the player. They would attack.

item.title: Games As Art (and thoughts on the Fallout TV Show)
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about games as art and the meaning of games, but it’s going to be a little rambling because I’m going to tell you how

I got to this topic. In the last couple weeks it’s the been the anniversary of two different friends of mine, Brian and Steve, passing away in the last within the last three years. They both died of cancer — different cancers. They both lived on opposite coasts of the US. Both of them I had met through online groups before we actually met in person and neither of them knew what I did for a living. But both of them were big video game players, and Steve especially, was excited when he found out what I’d done because he was a huge, Fallout fan. He would play and replay the Fallout games, and he would never be a bad person. In fact, if he ever did anything that led to a bad result, he would reload an earlier save game and play again, because he wanted everybody to be happy, and he wanted to be nice, and he was like that in real life too, but I just remember just how much he loved the games, and so it was not a surprise to me, uh, when his uh widower, Matthew, sent me a link to the recent uh photos that leaked from The Fallout TV show, you know, and he wanted to know what I thought of it, and, of course, I immediately shared it with Leonard, and to say we picked through these photos is probably an understatement: um, but it’s interesting because the way we went through them were very different um.

item.title: The Six Demos Of Fallout
item.description:
Hello everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about these six demos of Fallout.

Someone left a comment, uh, last week, and asked about something about the Fallout demo and I almost said which one? Because I remember just doing a couple. Remember, this is we’re going on almost 30 years ago that Fallout started. It started in 94, and I could remember a couple, but you know I’ve taken all my notes for Fallout, all the paper notes from Fallout are now digitized. So I have 86 pages of notes and this is with not one note per page.

item.title: Fallout: What I'd Change Today
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about what changes I would make to Fallout if I were to Tinker with it today.

I want to make this clear: I wouldn’t make big changes. I’m not talking about let’s remake Fallout as 3D, real time action game. No, I’m talking about, if not a remaster, but just if I was going to go back and go, God, if I had just had a little more time, it wouldn’t have shipped like this. So what would I have gone through and done?

item.title: Fallout Random Encounters
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Fallout, Random Encounters and exactly how those things work.

So several people have asked how they work and what controls them. So as you’re moving across the world map in Fallout, there’s a chance, based on the time you’re on the world map, that you will have an encounter and it will drop you onto a map. Now your Outdoorsman skill determines how far you can travel per time unit. So the higher your Outdoorsman skill, the farther you travel. So when you’re picking two points of travel in Outdoorsman makes it less likely that you will have an encounter because you’re putting in more miles per day. Similarly, Pathfinder, the perk says for any particular unit of time, the amount of gain time uh that passes is lower. So it reduces encounters as well by saying: you know, Outdoorsman says every day you travel more miles, Pathfinder says it takes you less part of a day to go those miles. So both of them reduce the time unit. That will be used to roll a random encounter. Now, regular encounters are based on the terrain and there are four train types in Fallout.

item.title: Fallout Criticals
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to explain exactly how criticals work in Fallout, and by criticals I mean both critical successes and critical failures.

This is sort of a follow-up, because someone asked about this when I explained how all the Fallout attributes work. So I want to go through exactly how we coded it. My notes are really clear on this, so I’ll go through um, just so you know.

item.title: A Chat With Scott Campbell
item.description: Tim, today I’m talking with Scott Campbell, the original lead designer, Fallout. We’re going to talk about Fallout things and what he’s been up to for the last forever. Yeah, seven years. Yeah, long long time, so you were the, you were one of the earliest team members on Fallout. You started on the team, I would think right after the two jasons. You know, uh, that was when I officially started. But uh, yeah, I mean since, like you know, even when we were just, like you know, playing gurps just for fun, after hours, you know I was, I was there, um, but even then, um, you know, I I have an actual trivia question for you, Tim. Do you remember the first time we met? Do you remember this?

item.title: Fallout SPECIAL Attribute Effects
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the attributes in Fallout, the special attributes.

Now I’m going to talk about exactly what they do. Part of the reason I want to do this is that almost a year ago, someone on Reddit was saying how they like games that have luck in them, and I explained what luck did in Fallout like in detail and I realized I had a document that about the time we shipped her shortly after, I wrote down exactly what all the attributes did, and I still have that document, um. But I know I had originally written it down on paper, so I went to go look for it. And what I was looking for, by the way, I found this: is a original Fallout shirt that still says gurps on it.

item.title: The Origin of Fallout's Setting
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the beginning, the creation of the origin of the Fallout setting.

If you remember what I said in my video about design documents, it all starts with setting, and I want to talk about that. What prompted me to think about this was, uh, somebody I know who’s in the industry — I’ve never worked with them — said I’d be interested to know more about how Fallout setting got created, and I thought I had mentioned it. But looking back, I’ve dropped little bits and pieces here and there in other videos, so I’m gonna try to make you a timeline of what happened. To create this timeline, I had to do a lot of research. I had both digital and actual packed away notes and why. Going through those boxes. That’s when I found this shirt, which I don’t think I’ve worn in five years, so minimum, maybe longer.

item.title: Fallout: Radiation vs FEV
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about radiation and the fev virus in Fallout and before I start I just want to say

this is my opinion. But if the opinion I had back then — and I even looked at my notes — I’m like, yep, that’s what I thought and it doesn’t really matter what I think because Canon is now set by Bethesda. But I also want to point out that even back then there was disagreement on the team over some of these, especially Goulds, which I will explain last, because I wanted, I want to walk through what my take on all of it was and what I had planned to do and make the and make Canon, starting in Fallout 2 and later.

item.title: Fallout's Voice Overs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about fallouts voice overs.

Now, what made me think of this was yesterday I had to do, I had to take part in some voice over sessions for another project I’m involved in and I happen to mention to someone there, and it was. This was done in Stockholm, so I ended up getting up like four in the morning so I could get on a zoom link to the rehearsal and I mentioned to some of the people there that I had not done this since Fallout 20, six years ago, 27 years ago and so this was kind of, you know, not new to me, but still somewhat novel.

item.title: A Chat With Jason Taylor
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I’m going to chat with Jason Taylor, JT, who was the first person on what would later be called team Fallout.

Hi, JT. Hey, how’s it going? Tim, I’m good. How are you? Great? It has been a long time you came to see the talk at GDC. Yeah, like 10 years ago.

You were in town and I was — I believe I was working in San Francisco at the time, uh — and then we met up and chatted. I saw your presentation was good, we had lunch afterwards, but I remember I didn’t know you’re going to be there and you were in the front and I was — like I have to stay honest now because he’s right there — like, yeah, I don’t remember you saying anything that made me want to jump out of my chair. So that’s why I told Leonard — this is why I like doing it, because I have my notes and I have everything. But a lot of this is perspective based and I find that sometimes I’ll leave a meeting with someone and we have a very different take on what someone literally just said. So, as, the person who started right away in the very like I got started in early 94 and then in Fall 94, you and Jason Anderson started I think the same week, but I think you actually started before him and you and I shared an office, yep, so I kind of wanted you to talk about very early. It was team gurps back then.

item.title: The Credits Problem (e.g. Fallout Companions)
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about credits and how people get credited in games, and I’m going to do that by telling you a story, because I’m frequently asked: hey uh, who did the companions in Fallout?

I love the companions. Let me tell a story, so when we first started designing Fallout in 1994.. There was a meeting where we were talking about how to do some fundamental design work and it came up: are we going to be playing one character? Are we going to have a party?

item.title: The First Time Fallout Was Almost Canceled
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk to you about the first time Fallout almost got canceled.

Now, what’s interesting about this? I don’t think I’ve ever talked about it publicly, but I don’t even think most people on the team know about this. Most people know about the whole gurp situation and that almost, you know, got the whole project Tangled Up.

Maybe I shouldn’t say cancel, but certainly almost installed, massively changed whatever. I have mentioned that when interplay got the DND license followed almost got canceled because they didn’t see the need to divert, devote resources to this Grade B product when they had DND that they called the license to print money. But there was one other time that it almost got canceled and it was because of me. So let me walk you through a story. So the spring of 95, Scott Everts was looking at open houses in the Orange County area. He decided he wanted to buy some place and he knew I had been.

item.title: The Biggest Influences On Fallout
item.description:
Hi. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about what influenced the creation of Fallout.

Now, I’ve already talked about — in fact, I have a whole video about — the influences on the music and I’m going to link that in the description. What I really want to talk about now is the: game itself, the setting, the story, individual elements. I’ve decided to kind of do it backwards. I I thought about this a few times. I was going to do the general influences and then talk about the specific. Instead, what I’m going to do is talk about the specific influences, going through elements of the game and tell you what influenced us, and then I’ll list Games, movies and books that had a more General influence, that there was no one particular thing we can point to and say, yeah, that’s from that movie or book. So I will start with companies, so vault tech, which plays a big role in Fallout and all the subsequent games.

item.title: The Sugar Bombs conspiracy...
item.description: Okay, people put on your foil conspiracy hats, as the skull Panda pointed out in the comments. Comments sugar bombs don’t appear as items in Fallout 1 and 2. But they do appear in my notes from the 90s and in reference to Calvin and Hobbes, the complete collection of which I own in the bookcase behind me. But they do appear in Fallout 3, New Vegas and for and Beyond. I have no explanation for this parallel design development. Mutual Calvin and Hobb fans.

item.title: GDC Showcase 2023
item.description: Hey everyone. I just wanted to announce that on Wednesday, June 28th at 1 pm, the GDC showcase is going to re-air my classic game postmortem on Fallout, and I will be doing a live chat at the same time, answering all the attendees Fallout related questions. There’s a link below, if you want to attend, thanks.

item.title: A Chat With Leonard Boyarsky, Part 2
item.description:
Him and I’m joined again today for part two of my chat with Leonard bararski. Hey Leonard, hey Tim, how’s it going? I’m good.

Before we get started, I saw that you, uh, showed your gnod trophy. I wanted to show my Award of Excellence that interplay gave me. Um, the funny thing about this is I think Jason got one too. There was a party — there’s a party — where all of interplay was at this party and they gave out that award. I don’t know if this is what happened. I think this is what happened with Gana too. You don’t remember what I’m gonna say, do you? So the next day, Rob comes in and he gives it to me. I’m like what is this?

item.title: Fallout Cut Content
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Fallout cut content.

This has been a very frequently Asked question um. It’s. People seem very fascinated by what didn’t make it into the game and I’m going to try to answer the question. But I’ve got a big caveat here. When I left interplay, it caused some consternation in the individuals above me and I was warned multiple times that I wasn’t allowed to take any interplay property you know off the site like I would have. So that meant I left all the code behind and I left all the design documents behind.

item.title: Frequently Asked Questions
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim.

The channel has now passed 15 000 subscribers. I’m still reading all the comments, but I have given up on replying to any of them. Um, I used to reply to some occasionally.

Part of it is there’s just too many coming in too fast, literally why? Why I was answering a question, another question would come in also. I’ve noticed that even when I’ve answered questions, people then ask the same question, sometimes in the same section. So what I decided to do is I gathered up a lot of questions have been answered or been asked multiple times and I’m going to answer them here. This is going to be my first fact. My first frequent answer asked questions.

item.title: A Chat With Leonard Boyarsky
item.description: I have my first guest, a man who needs no introduction: no, it’s Leonard burarski. I’ve known him for 30 years. We’ve been working together for much of that time — not all of it. He had his own Adventures and he looks exactly the same, and I look like a picture of Dorian Gray, so that is. That is verifiably untrue. People can go Google and make their own decision.

item.title: Why I Left Fallout 2
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today let’s talk about Fallout 2 and why I left it I really think, if you haven’t, you should watch yesterday’s video.

There’s a lot of context for this video in yesterday’s video about how Fallout was a b-tier project. Now I know I mentioned in that video that we had never really planned on a sequel, and by that what I meant was there were no hooks in the original story or the setting or even the system mechanics. I mean, we hadn’t planned on what would happen if the level went past 20. We just weren’t thinking of a sequel. We’re just trying to get Fallout 1 out the door. I think the only two times sequel even came up during development of Fallout 1 was during the naming meeting, where I even said vault 13 isn’t a good name because it doesn’t make for good sequels: vault 13, 2 or more vault 13 or vault 14.. And then again when we finish the manual for Fallout one, which was done.

item.title: Fallout Was A B-Tier Project
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim.

I’ve gotten a lot of questions about Fallout 2 and also especially why I left Fallout 2 in mid development. I think I’m ready to talk about that, but I’ll do that tomorrow, because to understand my answer requires understanding a particular context about Fallout. The first one, the base game. Some people call it Fallout one. I just I can’t call it Fallout one. It’s Fallout and what I want to. So what I want to talk about today is Fallout was a b tier project at interplay. I don’t know why some people from interplay deny this.

item.title: History of Fallout: The GURPS Apps
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I wanted to do a little bit of video game archeology or paleontology, I don’t know — demon ontology, because

I’ve been digging through my archives which unfortunately only go back to 90- 91. So I’ve lost most of what I did at my first, the first game company I worked at in Virginia, uh, Pegasus software, which turned into cyberon, where I made grand slam Bridge for Electronic Arts. I didn’t take any notes on that, but before Fallout at interplay and before the game I made before Fallout, rags to riches, and before I was even an employee at interplay, I was a contractor in 91 making Bard’s tail construction set. I was playing gurps a lot with my friends at UCI, where I was still enrolled in the PHD program which I was quickly becoming less enamored with. I wanted to start playing gurps at interplay. There were a lot of people there playing second edition ad, which I wasn’t really that into. In fact, on all these, books here, I think second edition is the only version of Dungeons and Dragons I don’t own, so I was. I wanted to teach them how to play groups which I was playing. Uh, I was playing with This is my original gurps third edition book and I was playing this at UCI and I was running a gurps space campaign. This is the old space book and there’s a chapter in here, chapter 10, which explains how to make star systems, and there’s a lot of roles and a lot of conditions and a lot of stuff that my Jello brain did not want to do.

item.title: How Fallout Got Its Name
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how Fallout got its name.

But before I do that, I need to tell you it’s very, very hard to come up with a name for a game, especially a new game with new mechanics and a new setting and new characters — characters - characters you would think you know there’s a whole team of creative people who’ve been working on it for a while. They can just go: boom, this is the name. It doesn’t work that way.

item.title: The Music of Fallout
item.description:
Hi. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about something that’s near and dear to me, which is fallout’s music.

I love ambient music. I have since College, when a fellow engineer friend of mine, Leila, let me listen to a Jean-Michel jar album and I was hooked. I bought all those albums. I bought brain Eno albums, I got into Patrick O’Hearn — just all the ambient stuff.

item.title: How Fallout was made SPECIAL
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how, in just two weeks, right near the end, about four or five months before Fallout shipped, we replaced gurps with special.

Now this isn’t going to be a video about why we had to do that — that’s another video, maybe, if I ever feel like talking about that. But what I want to talk about is: have we managed to replace such an integral part of what you would think would be a game so quickly? And there’s really two reasons for that that I’ll get into. But they were that Chris Taylor, our lead designer, already had several Homebrew systems and they had one that looked really good and I’ll tell you how, what that was and how we integrated it. And then the other reason we could replace, uh, the system mechanics so quickly was the nature of how Fallout came to be, and I know — I’ve talked and you may have read other places, that Fallout started very small, uh, just me, and then I was really championing Gertz. So let me talk about both of those.

item.title: The True Purpose of Vaults in Fallout
item.description: Hi everyone, it’s me Tim. Wow, you are filling up my comments with so many good ideas for topics that I can’t even really keep up. I had planned today, uh, to either talk about Bloodlines, vampire Bloodlines and the cut content and some of our plans for the sequels, and then I thought, no, no, I’m gonna talk about music, and not just the great music in Fallout, but arcanum’s music and vampires music and the fact that I’m sad each and every day that I got to work with the composer, Johan Johansson.

item.title: The Origin of Dumb Dialog
item.description:
Hi. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to tell a story.

One of my favorite stories. It’s the origin of dumb dialogue in Fallout, and not even just in Fallout, I mean.

Once we used it there, we did an Arcana we kept using. It most recently appeared in The Outer Worlds.

In fact, one of the narrative designers in a taipadar came to me one day and said: you know, dumb dialogue’s a lot of work, but whenever I get stuck I just think what would Tim say? I think he meant that as a compliment, anyway.

item.title: Let's Talk Fallout 1
item.description:
Hot, hi, it’s me Tim. Uh, before I start, um, that power armor helmet was made by Josh J, an artist on Fallout 4, and he sent it to me. How cool is that? Uh, yeah, it barely fits over my big head though. Um, it’s really fun though. It’s cool to have something like that.

Thank you, Josh. Um, so the real Jacob 603 asked me some Fallout questions on the last video, so I thought I’d answer them in a video format rather than in text, and I’m going to call this — let’s talk — Fallout one — CU. I have a feeling Fallout questions will come up a lot, um, so he asked me what Fallout, what I think of the modern fallouts and the whole Fallout series and the new ones. Let me just caveat: I’ve never played Fallout, Brotherhood of Ste or um Fallout 76, and I barely played any Fallout tactics. Uh, mostly I didn’t play Brotherhood steel because when it came out um, I was super busy. Same thing with tactics um Fallout 76. Um, I think I understand it’s a great game. It’s just it’s online multiplayer, um mostly having to do with Base building, and those aren’t for me. So and, by the way, if, a game comes out and it has features you don’t like, don’t call it a stupid game.
